data.title: RIV: Fund Of Funds With An Appealing Distribution Yield
data.description:
The RiverNorth Opportunities Fund (RIV) is a closed-end fund designed to enhance distribution income for investors by investing in closed-end funds, exchange-traded funds, special purpose acquisition companies [SPACs], and business development companies [BDCs]. The strategy has a forward distribution rate of $1.53/share, yielding 13.38%.

About RiverNorth Opportunities Fund

RIV was launched on December 24, 2015, by RiverNorth Capital Management as a multi-asset strategy designed to optimize income for investors. The strategy takes a diversified approach to allocating assets by selecting managed strategies and SPACs to drive growth and income, allocating across equities, fixed income, and BDCs. RIV has a management fee of 177 bps and a number of other fees for a total expense ratio of 669 bps.

The fund has weighting caps to be used as guardrails for investment rotation:

  • Fixed Income, Funds, & SPACs: 60%
  • Global Equities: 30%
  • Emerging Market Equities: 15%
  • Real Estate: 10%
  • Energy Master Limited Partnerships [MLPs]: 15%

Within fixed income, no more than 30% of the total assets can be allocated to high-yield or senior loans. CEFs held in the strategy are selected using quantitative analysis, primarily to select funds that are trading at a discount to the net asset value [NAV] with the expectation of the discount narrowing. The fund may also utilize total return swaps to enhance returns in the portfolio with a weight cap of 15%.

According to RIV’s most recent annual report, CEFs accounted for 59.64% of the total portfolio weight. The top holding within the asset class was Pershing Square Holdings at 12.04%. Pershing Square Holdings largely invests in large-cap companies, with its top holdings including Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), and Brookfield Corporation (BN). Interestingly enough, RIV had a short position in BN per its last annual report.

SPACs were the second largest allocation in RIV, making up 40.38% of the total portfolio weight. The largest holding within the SPACs category was the PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund (PDX) [per the annual report], though it is an income-oriented global credit markets fund. The largest SPAC holding was the Ares Acquisition Corporation II at 1.8%. At the end of September 2025, the company de-SPACed as Kodiak AI (KDK), an AI-powered autonomous vehicle technology company. KDK has since traded down from its initial trading price with an enterprise value of $1.22b, well below its $2.5b valuation prior to going public.

Since its annual report, RIV has reduced its exposure to SPACs from roughly 40% down to 28%, likely to derisk the portfolio while focusing on other assets like BDCs, ETFs, and debt.

On October 8, 2025, RIV announced the issuance of 5.9mm new shares of common stock at 92.5% of net asset value per share, raising $70.5mm.

RIV pays out an appealing monthly distribution rate annualized at $1.53/share, yielding 13.38% on a forward basis. Distributions have held steady for the last few years following a rate cut at the beginning of 2023. A large proportion of the fund’s distributions have historically been a return of capital [ROC].

ROC is considered a non-dividend distribution. ROC has a tax-deferred benefit in which it will lower the cost basis of the investment until it reaches $0/share; from there, ROC will be taxed as ordinary income. This poses a dilemma for investors in determining which account RIV will be most suitable for; non-qualified dividends will be taxed at ordinary rates. I believe whether RIV is placed in a taxable or tax-advantaged account will be entirely up to the investor and whether they plan on using the income more immediately.

Though RIV provides an appealing multi-asset strategy, investors may consider peer strategies that may offer similar exposure and high-yielding benefits.

Comparing historical performance, the Special Opportunities Fund (SPE) and Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunities Fund (FOF) have each outperformed RIV during most holding periods on a total return basis.

In addition to this, investors may consider ETFs like the Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS) or the Calamos CEF Income & Arbitrage ETF (CCEF) as ETF alternatives to CEFs. For performance and fees, FOF may be one of the more appealing strategies as an alternative to RIV.

Risks Related to RIV

RIV is a multi-asset CEF designed to provide investors with growth & income. RIV’s investments in SPACs can be considered as speculative, as many companies that go public through this method may not be as high of quality as ones that enter the public markets through an initial public offering. CEFs trading at a discount present the risk of remaining at a discount and never trading at NAV.

Final Thoughts

RIV can provide investors with a broad range of assets through investment in CEFs, SPACs, BDCs, as well as other financial instruments that may generate income. In terms of making an investment decision, peer strategies like SPE or FOF may be more optimal investment opportunities given their historical track records and more moderate fees. Given these factors, I am recommending RIV with a Hold rating.

data.title: Analog Devices: An AI Infrastructure Enabler
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Analog Devices Inc. (ADI) is not your typical semiconductor company. It is actually one of my high-conviction pick as an AI infrastructure enabler. And this is why I am rating it A Buy because its high performance analog and power products sit inside the very AI systems, data centers, and automated factories that are now seeing the biggest capex growth. It is quietly powering the secular boom in AI, automation, and next-gen data centers. My investment thesis on ADI is quite straightforward: ADI's high performance analog, mixed signal and power solutions are quite critical to AI and ML systems, advanced robotics, Autonomous vehicles [AV] and cutting-edge industrial applications. And as these trends accelerate, ADI monetizes the surge in demand for precision signal processing and power management. All this is done while leveraging a resilient and a diversified business model. As CEO, Vincent Roche, said that

...Despite the geopolitical and macro turbulence, we remain undeterred and excited by the tremendous growth opportunities that we see over both the near and long terms....

And given that the company combined secular growth tailwinds with efficient cash generation and profitability, it gives me confidence that the company warrants a Buy rating.

Business Overview

Analog Devices Inc. is a leader in high-performance analog and mixed-processing, signal semiconductors. It has a strong franchise in power management and signal chain processing, which is basically the picks and shovels that bridge the physical and digital worlds. The company's chips convert real world analog signals such as sound, pressure, motion and power into digital data with extreme accuracy. ADI boasts 6 decades of domain expertise and a vast catalog of precision components. From sensors and data converters to RF, power ICs, and embedded processors. As I said earlier, ADI is a highly diversified business across end markets, and industrial and instrumentation is the largest segment because it takes about 50% of revenue. Then there is automotive, which does about 30% of revenues, and communication, which takes another 15%. And finally the last segment is taken by consumer electronics. It is important to mention that about 85% of ADI revenues come from Industrial, Auto and Comms and this provides some level of stability in terms of sales. I would say that the diversification in products has helped off, especially during the recent downturn. I also note that ADI enjoys a premium performance moat in its niche, as also highlighted by management:

...we tend to get a premium for our performance, and that's the innovation premium you've all heard me talk about, where performance matters...

This tends to make it hard for lower-end competitors to displace ADI. As a result, this combination of breadth and technical depth gives ADI a durable edge, especially as we now enter a new investment cycle in AI and automation.

Let's now look at some of the growth and profitability metrics for ADI.

Growth and Profitability

The company's latest results blew past expectations in Q3 2025. I say this because ADI had endured one of the longest down cycles in memory, as shown below. And Q3 2025 earnings report illustrated the sharp recovery. Revenues jumped to $2.88Bn, representing an increase of 25% y/y.

The industrial segment increased by 23% as factory automation and instrumentation bounced back strongly. Automotive also grew by 22% y/y. And lastly, Communications increased by over 40% due to AI related demand for cloud infrastructure. Even consumer electronics, which traditionally have been a weaker spot for ADI, increased by 21%. I note that ADI scored a C- growth grade as industrial sales started climbing each quarter since the trough last year. CFO Rich Puccio noted that industrial orders are growing across all the sub-sectors and geographies, and this is clear evidence that virtually all segments are in growth mode simultaneously.

I note that ADI maintained its excellent profitability through the cycle. I note that the company scored an excellent A+ profitability grade. And this is because the margins are now on an upswing. Last quarter's gross margin was 69.2%, and the operating margin stood at 42.2%. And both of them improved by 100 bps y/y in Q3 2025. Also management expects gross margins to recover to 70% as factory utilization normalizes and the rich industrial mix returns. Looking ahead, I believe ADI's operating margin will trend between 40% with volume growth, even accounting for higher R&D and normalizing incentive compensation.

The company is also a cash machine. This is because over the past 12 months, ADI generated $3.7Bn of FCFs. Yet management returned over $3.5Bn to shareholders in that period as dividends and buybacks. And this reiterates a policy of 100% FCFs return over the long term.

Now that we have gone through the growth and profitability of ADI, let's look at some of the growth drivers that I believe will drive ADI's stock price higher.

Growth Drivers

I believe ADI sits at the nexus of multiple secular growth trends. First and foremost, the AI data center boom is a an ongoing explosion in AI model training and cloud services that is driving massive capital investments in data centers. And ADI is quietly one of the winners behind the scenes. Yet every hyperscale server rack requires high speed optical connectivity and efficient power deliver. And this is exactly, what ADI provides. I have previously talked about optical connectivity in one of my articles titled "Marvell Technology Is Positioned Squarely At The Center Of The AI Trend" Please refer to get a better understanding on the same. Nevertheless, ADI's management has noted that there is "no letup" in AI spending patterns:

...Similarly, on the ATE side, there's no -- it appears to be no letup in the AI/data center CapEx spending patterns, which we also think helps us on the ATE side....

And this benefits ADI's broad portfolio. For example, ADI's optical transceivers and DSP chips enable the 800G+ connections between AI accelerator clusters, and its power management ICs maximize energy efficiency in server boards. These optical and power building blocks play a similar role to what digital networking vendors provide on the ASIC side, but ADI's revenue is spread across many OEMs and platforms instead of a few hyperscale design wins.

In Q3 2025, the comm segments accounted for 13% of revenue and grew about 4% sequentially and 37% y/y as AI driven cloud and networking demand continue to build. The company also scored 2 major design wins for next-gen cloud hardware. And this will begin generating revenue in 2026. These wins reinforce that ADI's content is growing at the cutting edge of AI infrastructure. And this simply means that as long as companies like Amazon (AMZN). Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META) are pouring billions into AI compute, and then ADI will be selling more high-performance components into those data centers.

Secondly, there is factory automation and robotics. As of now there is a new wave of industrial automation, which is driven by labor shortages, according to McKinsey & Company. Remember, this is a core ADI territory, as I had mentioned earlier; therefore, I'd expect substantial growth from this, given that automation orders have increased in the last 2 orders. The real excitement is actually the future of robotics, which I believe ADI is well positioned for, given its core strength is in industry and automation.

Lastly, I'd say it's automotive electrification and ADAS. The auto industry's march towards electric vehicles and AV is another multiyear secular driver for ADI. Auto already represents 30% of ADI's revenue, and it has proven to be remarkably resilient.

Valuation

In regard to valuation, I note that the company scores a D valuation grade as shown below. Nevertheless, I note that the FWD Non-GAAP PEG stands at 1.56x while the sector median stands at 1.60x, implying that the stock is trading at a slight discount relative to the sector.

When I look at FWD Non-GAAP P/E, I note that ADI is trading at 29.91x while the sector median stands at 22.46x. For context, Texas Instruments (TXN) trades around 28x FWD Non-GAAP P/E, while NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) trades at about 16.24x, Microchip Technology (MCHP) at 35x, Marvel (MRVL) at 29x, and Broadcom (AVGO) about 50x. From an FWD Non-GAAP P/E perspective, I would say that ADI is trading at a slight discount to its peers with the exception of NXPI

Lastly in terms of the FWD EV/EBITDA multiple, I note that ADI stands at approximately 25x, while the sector median stands at 13x. Texas Instruments (TXN) for instance, is about 19x EBITDA, and NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) is at 12x. Marvel Technology (MRVL) is at 23x. ADI's multiple is a bit higher, reflecting its temporarily depressed EBITDA from the recent downturn and its superior margin profile. And this means that ADI is trading at a premium to peers by FWD EV/EBITDA. I expect this gap to narrow as ADI's EBITDA accelerates with the cyclical recovery.

Relative to Texas Instruments, ADI trades on a similar FWD P/E but leans a little more into high performance industrial, aerospace, and AI-driven communications, while TXN still skews more towards broad industrial and embedded. Versus NXPI, which is heavily tied to autos and trades at a lower multiple, I view ADI as the higher quality compounder with a broader end market mix and higher FCF margins. Compared to Marvell Technology, which is a pure digital networking and custom ASIC play for AI data centers, ADI's multiple is in the same ballpark, yet ADI delivers structurally higher gross margins and less earnings volatility because of its industrial and automotive base. And in my view, that balance of AI upside and defensive cash generation justifies ADI's current premium to the sector median and supports my Buy rating.

I would say that ADI's valuation is reasonable. And this is because with an approximate 20% EPS growth combined with a 70% gross margin on sale for 29X FWD Non-GAAP P/E. Given the company's technology leadership and improving outlook, I see this as a valuation disconnect that provides an attractive entry point. And hence the reason for assigning it a Buy rating.

ADI's Key Risks

The biggest risk I see for ADI as of now is macro downcycle risk. A global economic slowdown or another semiconductor inventory correction could hit ADI's near term sales and delay its recovery. There is also a risk that Geopolitical uncertainty could hurt ADI's revenues. For instance, export restrictions on China or tariff escalations could impact ADI's sizable international business. Given that industrial and automation forms a huge segment of ADI's revenues, and a huge portion of its supply chain comes from other countries such as China, then there is a definite risk that a disruption in the supply chain could really hurt ADI''s sales.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, ADI has positioned itself as a diversified player riding powerful secular trends in AI, automation, EV, and connectivity. ADI's role as an AI infrastructure enabler puts the company in a sweet spot to benefit from the capex upcycle of the coming years. And remember, I have demonstrated that all the diversified engines are all firing now. From factories to Cloud and EVs. And most importantly, management is executing well with financial discipline. And this is why I am giving ADI a Buy rating.

data.title: PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Core Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. equities notched record highs to end the quarter, primarily driven by easing monetary policy, strong corporate earnings, and artificial intelligence (AI) hype.

Russell 2000 small-cap stocks rallied substantially, experiencing their best quarter since 2021.

The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut its target rate by 0.25% for the first time in nine months, prompted by a weakening labor market.

U.S. equities posted positive returns across the board for the quarter, with the Russell 2000 returning more than 12%, and the S&P 500 and S&P 400 indexes returning 8.12% and 5.55%, respectively.

Factor performance was mixed to start the quarter. However, value experienced a notable rebound in August across all market-cap segments, boosting relative returns. Growth as a style generally outperformed value stocks, while quality factors underperformed.

Quarter

After a volatile second quarter marked by unfolding global trade tensions, U.S. equities climbed to new record highs during the third quarter amid a calmer environment. The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) returned to low-risk levels and remained well below the historical average. Investors were in “risk-on” mode, looking past the realities of continued stretched valuations and above-target inflation. The significant market rally during the quarter was widespread, with gains extending beyond mega-cap tech.

Easing global trade tensions contributed to the stable market landscape. The U.S. reached trade deal agreements with key trading partners such as Japan and the European Union in July, ahead of the month-end deadline. However, the Supreme Court will soon consider a challenge to reciprocal tariffs implemented by the current administration.

All eyes were on the Fed during the quarter as economic data sent mixed signals. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) grew 3.8% during the second quarter, following a 0.6% decline in the first quarter, offering conflicting signals about the health of the economy. The latest unemployment rate was 4.3% for August, which, although low by historical standards, has steadily increased since the beginning of the year. Coupled with a slowdown in job growth, signs point to a softening labor market. Inflation remains sticky, with recent readings above the Fed's 2% target. By mid-August, investors became undoubtedly optimistic that rate cuts were imminent after Fed Chair Powell's dovish comments. Ultimately, the Fed cut its target rate by 0.25% during its September meeting, its first cut in nine months. Current projections indicate two additional rate cuts this year.

The period was unique for small-cap equities. Following a bearish start to the year, small-cap stocks outperformed large-cap stocks, returning over 12% for the quarter. The outperformance was fueled by optimism surrounding easing monetary policy, as small-cap companies stand to reap the benefits of lower borrowing costs.

During the month of July, investors ventured into low-quality, speculative stocks, prompting a junk rally that proved to be short lived. Unprofitable companies in the small-cap space outperformed their positive earning counterparts by over 9% in September. This was most notable in the Information Technology sector, a phenomenon last observed in 2020.

Though the third quarter market rally was broad, mega-cap tech stocks remained the front-runners, driven by AI enthusiasm and strong earnings results. The Russell 1000 Growth Index returned over 10%, compared to around 5% for the Russell 1000 Value Index. The valuation disparity widened for the quarter, with the forward-looking Price-to-Earnings (P/E) multiple of the Russell 1000 Growth Index rising above 35, almost double that of the Value Index.

Factor performance was mixed for the period. Value and quality factors struggled, particularly in the small-cap space during July when low-quality, speculative stocks outperformed. However, August was a particularly strong month for our strategies. Value experienced a meaningful rebound, as investors rotated into undervalued parts of the market. Quality performance also improved markedly in tandem with value, boosting relative returns, before detracting again in September, most notably in the small-cap space. Growth factor performance was mixed to start the quarter, but outperformed value handily in September. Overall, the majority of our core strategies saw positive relative performance for the quarter. Nevertheless, uncertainty lies ahead, notably from global tensions and the pace of Fed easing.

Outlook and Positioning

PGIM Quantitative Solutions employs a quantitative, bottom-up investment process. Our proprietary stock selection model utilizes a carefully chosen suite of value, growth and quality factors with strong theoretical underpinnings to evaluate a broad universe of stocks. Our process is adaptive. Factors are dynamically weighted to reflect differences in individual stock growth rates. This ensures we are examining stocks through the right lenses.

Using the relative scores from the stock selection model, PGIM Quantitative Solutions constructs a Fund that efficiently targets factor exposures, while minimizing unintended risks and transactions costs. The result is a diversified core Fund that is positioned to produce consistent alpha over time.

data.title: Vale: A Long-Term Buy Despite Near-Term Noise
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Vale (VALE), I highlighted their strong margins and significant long-term growth potential in energy transition metals, with strong fundamentals and ramp-ups that can support their long-term potential.

With a good report released recently, solid fundamentals, continued investments, and the same strong long-term tailwinds outweighing the near-term headwinds and potential for more lawsuit issues, Vale remains a Buy for me.

Internal Developments

As we can see, Vale reported a very solid FCF in Q3 (~$2.57 billion), reaching a FCF of $4.08 billion over the first 9 months of 2025. Still, Vale is expecting Q4 to come with higher CAPEX, expecting $5.4 billion to $5.7 billion for the whole year and spending only $3.78 billion in 9M'25, so there's $1.62 billion to $1.82 billion left for Q4.

Financially, based on Vale's latest report, we see a solid position, with the current assets covering the current liabilities and not that much debt. Still, we see the debt increasing quite a bit since Vale now has a higher net debt target, allowing them to invest and expand at better rates while taking advantage of the current environment.

A very important risk to keep in mind that the market may be overlooking now is that BHP (BHP) - analyzed recently and rated a Buy - recently lost a new legal battle in the UK related to the 2015 Brazil dam collapse (i.e. Brazil's worst environmental disaster, which BHP and Vale also lost in Brazil a few years ago), in a lawsuit brought by over 600,000 Brazilian people, businesses, and local governments, with the company making a new $500 million worth of provisions following this. You may be inclined to think that this is only for BHP, but BHP had a "contribution claim" against Vale for this case as well that was later replaced by an agreement in July 2024, with both of them agreeing to pay 50-50 for anything related to the UK/Dutch group actions.

Still, since they already settled this in Brazil and paid (and will continue to pay) compensation based on an approved long-term plan, BHP expects this to "significantly reduce the size and value of claims in the U.K. group action," with the new ruling citing that "BHP are strictly liable as polluters in respect of damage caused by the collapse" under environmental law, not under corporate law - potentially exposing them to even more costs (which they'll appeal), so this issue is not yet over.

Macro Developments

Something important to keep in mind is that Brazil's interest rates were maintained around the same levels, some of the highest since the early 2000s. The country's Central Bank expects to maintain the SELIC rate at around 15% and enter into a "very prolonged pause" at this level, but we should keep in mind that when they are ready to cut them and continue the long-term trend of lower and lower rates, this can boost Vale in several ways.

First of all, this would lower their cost of debt, which is already generally low since Brazil's companies get some preferred rates from BNDES (Banco Nacional de Desenvolvimento Econômico e Social), which can be excellent, especially now since they've increased their net debt limits. Rate cuts can also boost the economy and incentivize investments thanks to it and supporting the country's demand for Vale's iron.

Plus, this would also lower the risk-free rate and lead to more interest for Brazil's high-yield stocks, especially with global rate cuts on top changing the risk-adjusted yields globally. Again, since Brazil has a mandatory minimum dividend of 25% of a company's profit and they generally have excellent profits, margins, and costs, the dividend will always be very solid, especially if the valuations remain low, so it's not hard to see how these yields can be attractive for the global markets once global rate cuts advance (see the US for example, where treasuries are still at a solid ~4% now).

But, something I noted in the Petrobras (PBR) analysis posted recently is that we've seen an important change coming from the Brazilian Senate approving the reinstatement of a 10% withholding tax on dividends paid to nonresident shareholders, which is part of Brazil's wider tax reform initiatives. The exact implications ultimately depend on your country of residency, so make sure you double-check for your particular situation. I don't really mind it, and I'm used to even bigger ones, but it's of course hurting the yield, so it can have an impact on the market's perceived value once it gets enforced.

Another important factor to take into account is the recent Trump-Xi deal, where the US and China would reportedly work towards easing the previously-imposed chip export ban, reopen the market for US farmers (which China had previously halted for many months, hurting US farmers), and more, potentially being good for the international markets since these two are key for global trade.

These are on top of the rollback on some tariffs, including Brazilian coffee, fruit, beef, and more, potentially leading the way for more improvements, although it's worth mentioning that Vale doesn't import that much to the US, but the country still has an impact on global prices.

On top of China's policy shifts that should be good for the long term, Vale recently saw potential from India, with rising demand from the country helping to offset the stagnant demand we've seen from China, with Vale's CEO expecting India's steel production capacity to even double in the following 5 to 7 years, albeit China will remain the top consumer and producer in these industries for ages, accounting for ~60% of Vale's sales.

Most things are advancing in an overall good direction for Vale since the previous analysis, but there are still several risks to remember in this case, especially if we think about the geopolitical environment. Trump's relationship with Brazil and BRICS remains quite tense, and the country ultimately plays a very big role in the global commodity market, which makes it a very volatile environment to be in.

But, similar to Petrobras, Vale has been going through this environment (and even worse) for many decades, and I'm glad to see that they're ramping up their investments and increasing the net debt target, taking advantage of more than just iron going forward.

Updated Valuation

Based on Vale's 9M results and CAPEX expectations, I'll start the valuation with $4.25 billion in FCF in 2025, expecting a 4.5% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to their ramped-up investments and copper's and nickel's potential, as well as the prospects of China's demand recovering in the future, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 thanks to their future-facing commodities, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate. Once again, I'll assume a $1.5 billion jump from 2027 onwards thanks to potentially better CAPEX and projects/production ramping up together with improved costs. I'll go for a WACC of 10.5% (above my estimated 9.88% to be a bit more conservative; assumptions: ~11.2% cost of equity, ~6.5% cost of debt), with $16.64 billion in expanded net debt (takes into account dam failure provisions), along with 4.27 billion shares outstanding based on the Q3 report. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $75.70 billion, an equity value of $59.06 billion, and an intrinsic value of $13.83 per share.

Again, I believe $4.25 billion as a base remains very low (even with the $1.5 billion injection) to assume as an average in this case, but it offers a good indicator of a relatively fair value below which the margin of safety would be great, with significantly higher potential in good times.

Conclusions

Vale remains a Buy in my opinion, with solid fundamentals, ramped-up investments, and a key role in our future thanks to their vital commodities, likely continuing to pay a very solid dividend over the long term. Risks remain, especially given the near-term headwinds and political uncertainty, but Vale is definitely a solid option to consider for a long-term-oriented portfolio.

data.title: Allspring High Yield Municipal Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The fund outperformed its benchmark, the High Yield Municipal Bond Blended Index, for the three-month period that ended September 30, 2025.
  • Duration, selection, and sector were the largest drivers of performance, while credit quality detracted.

Market review

Municipal returns were positive (3.00%) and outperformed taxable fixed income asset classes, with the Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Bond Index returning 2.51%, Treasuries 1.51%, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index 2.03%, corporates 2.60%, and U.S. high yield 2.54%. Within municipal credit tiers, A-rated bonds performed best (3.08%) and high yield bonds were the weakest (1.63%). The yield on the U.S. Treasury 10-year note ended September at 4.16%, down 8 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) over the quarter and down 42 bps from the start of 2025. U.S. equity markets were positive for the quarter.

With the downward movement of the municipal curve, shorter-end municipal-to-Treasury ratios decreased during the quarter while longer ratios remain cheap. Municipal 2-year, 5-year, and 10-year ratios of 64%, 62%, and 70%, respectively, are rich, while the 30-year ratio of 90% remains the cheapest portion of the tax-exempt market relative to taxable fixed income.

Net inflows were $11 billion for the quarter, and gross issuance was approximately $157 billion (a 3% quarter-over-quarter decrease), with tax-exempt issuance of $150 billion (a 0.5% quarter-over-quarter increase).

In September, the Federal Open Market Committee lowered its benchmark interest rate to 4.00–4.25%. The municipal benchmark yield curve moved downward, with rates on the short end declining by as much as 36 bps and longer rates by as much as 30 bps. Market expectations favor at least one additional cut this year, though the size and timing of Federal Reserve action remains uncertain. The rate environment reinforces the case for municipal bonds as a stable, income-generating asset amid ongoing market volatility.

Without this cap, the fund's returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Duration—contributor

  • The fund's strong overweight to bonds in the 12- to 15-year duration category was accretive to performance, as this category outperformed the index. Our underweights to the 15-year-plus category offset some of this contribution, as this category performed strongly.
  • Municipal bond returns were positive for the quarter. We expect record issuance to continue through the end of the year and to outpace fund flows, which points to a weaker technical environment for municipals. Flows remained positive throughout the quarter, with year-to-date inflows at $29 billion, though total inflows remain below historical averages. We remain bullish on municipals because absolute rates represent a compelling entry point, and over the near term, we expect income to be the driver for the asset class. With the recent yield curve volatility, we expect to maintain duration closer to neutral, and we see opportunities in the intermediate portion of the curve and expect to be selective about adding duration.

Yield curve—contributor

  • Returns were positive across all maturity segments for the benchmark except for maturities from 2 to 4 years in the index. Significant underweights to bonds between 8 and 22 years detracted from performance, as these categories strongly outperformed the index. However, our considerable overweight to bonds in the 22-year-plus category offset this underperformance as this category also outperformed.
  • The municipal benchmark curve shifted downward during the third quarter, with rates falling by 19–36 bps across the curve, and the spread of 186 bps between the 1-year and 30-year Municipal Market Data (MMD) tightened, down from 197 bps at the beginning of the quarter. We remain attentive to pockets of weakness or opportunity that may arise. With only 24 bps between 20-year and 30-year MMD, the continued compression makes extremely long-dated exposure less attractive for investors. In our view, the risk/reward trade-off strongly favors taking a more measured approach to curve allocations.

Quality—contributor

  • Investment-grade and B-rated bonds outperformed the index. Our overweight to BB-rated and non-rated bonds detracted from performance, as these categories underperformed. Additionally, our underweight to AAA and AA rated bonds detracted from performance, as these categories outperformed. Some of this underperformance was offset by our overweight to A and BBB rated credits.
  • Credit spreads remain tight. Despite the rate volatility, the spread between AAA and high yield credits remains near the tightest levels over the past two years, making credit relatively more expensive. Looking ahead, we believe credit fundamentals are well supported by the economy, though the possibility of credit spread widening remains ever present. There are opportunities for more risk-taking investors to explore the high yield space; however, as the July default by Brightline Florida demonstrates, security selection remains crucial in this segment of the market.

Sector—contributor

  • We continue to be significantly overweight revenue bonds and modestly underweight general obligation (GO) bonds. Controlling for duration, curve, and quality allocation, contributors to relative performance included an overweight allocation to the hospital, local GO, and education sectors. Some of this contribution was offset by underweights to the water and sewer and special tax sectors.
  • In times of economic uncertainty, GO bonds backed by strong income and sales tax receipts along with essential services provided by municipalities are generally more defensive than discretionary sectors. We remain focused on these areas while also seeking opportunities in higher-beta revenue sectors where price dislocations create value. These dynamics underscore the need for an experienced team with institutional bandwidth to navigate an evolving market.

Issue selection—contributor

  • Controlling for duration, curve, quality, and sector allocation effects, security selection was modestly accretive for the quarter. Relative to the benchmark, the largest contribution effects were seen in the transportation, industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue, and education sectors. Selection within the housing, leasing, and special tax sectors was negative. The fund's underexposure to Puerto Rico was a detractor from performance, while underexposure to Florida Transportation was accretive to performance.
  • The volatility observed in the first nine months of the year serves as a reminder to bond investors that municipals are credit products and not just a rates asset class. Security selection remains critical, as certain credits may diverge from the broader market due to forced selling, liquidity constraints, and structural factors. These inefficiencies can lead to atypical execution and present opportunities for strong risk-adjusted returns among liquidity providers. Prudent liquidity management has been a hallmark of our investment process, enabling us to capitalize on ongoing market dislocations while identifying compelling relative value opportunities.

Definition of terms:

30-day SEC yield: The 30-day SEC yield is calculated with a standardized formula mandated by the SEC. The formula is based on maximum offering price per share and includes the effect of any fee waivers. Without waivers, yields would be reduced. The 30-day unsubsidized SEC yield does not reflect waivers in effect. A fund's actual distribution rate will differ from the SEC yield, and any income distributions from the fund may be higher or lower than the SEC yield.

Average maturity: The average maturity represents the weighted average time to maturity of all the debt securities held in the portfolio. A relatively short average maturity results in smaller price fluctuations in response to changes in market rates of interest. A short average maturity subjects the owner of a debt portfolio to the risk that maturing debt will be replaced with debt carrying a lower interest rate. Average maturity is an important consideration for investors who hold bond and money market funds.

Alpha: measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta: measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Credit-quality ratings: Credit-quality ratings apply to underlying holdings of the fund and not the fund itself. Standard & Poor's and Fitch rate the creditworthiness of bonds from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Standard & Poor's rates the creditworthiness of short-term notes from SP-1 (highest) to SP-3 (lowest). Ratings from A to CCC may be modified by the addition of a plus (+) or minus (-) sign to show relative standing within the rating categories. Moody's rates the creditworthiness of bonds from Aaa (highest) to CC (lowest). Ratings Aa to B may be modified by the addition of a number 1 (highest) to 3 (lowest) to show relative standing within the ratings categories. Moody's rates the creditworthiness of short-term U.S. tax-exempt municipal securities from MIG-1/VMIG-1 (highest) to SG (lowest). Credit quality and credit-quality ratings are subject to change.

Duration: Duration is a measurement of the sensitivity of a bond's price to changes in Treasury yields. A fund's duration is the weighted average of duration of the bonds in the portfolio. Duration should be interpreted as the approximate change in a bond's (or fund's) price for a 100-basis-point change in Treasury yields. Duration is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Yield curve: The yield curve is a graphical representation of fixed-income security yields (usually U.S. Treasuries) at their respective maturities, starting with the shortest time to maturity and sequentially plotting in a line chart to the longest maturity. The yield curve is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Risks: All investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Bond values fluctuate in response to the financial condition of individual issuers, general market and economic conditions, and changes in interest rates. Changes in market conditions and government policies may lead to periods of heightened volatility in the bond market and reduced liquidity for certain bonds held by the fund. In general, when interest rates rise, bond values fall and investors may lose principal value. Interest rate changes and their impact on the fund and its share price can be sudden and unpredictable. High-yield securities have a greater risk of default and tend to be more volatile than higher-rated debt securities. The use of derivatives may reduce returns and/or increase volatility. A portion of the fund's income may be subject to federal, state, and/or local income taxes or the alternative minimum tax. Any capital gains distributions may be taxable. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risk.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

data.title: SPHY: My HY Passive Pick, But Watch Out For The OAS
data.description:
When I think of a passive investment solution in the US high-yield segment, I immediately think of the State Street® SPDR® Portfolio High Yield Bond ETF (SPHY). It remains a well-constructed solution that fits into a very competitive market, with constantly new proposals.

Its strength? Certainly a competitive expense ratio, which in this sector is known to have a significant weight on total returns. But also its structure, with a contained duration and the prevalence of BB solutions.

Yet there is an element that deserves to be scrutinized before giving an overall judgment to the category: the Option-Adjusted Spread (OAS) issue. In recent years, the compression of the spread has stimulated the overall growth of the ETF. Today, however, the underlying trend of this indicator seems to have reversed, and the upward trend of the OAS is not a very positive signal for the high-yield segment.

And the question I ask myself is: given the ongoing inversion process, could it in turn work against it?

My opinion is partly yes.

But first …

What is SPHY

The fund replicates the ICE BofA US High Yield Index, which measures the performance of US corporate bonds below investment grade. And it is a change compared to the past, considering that since 2019 it replicated the ICE BofAML US Diversified Crossover Corporate Index (a HY/IG crossover index). The fund also had another name, until 2019 it was called CJNK. And then it changed to the current SPHY.

In terms of costs, it has a very low expense ratio for a high yield, equal to 0.05% and I must confess that, considering a 30-day Bid/Ask spread of 0.04%, this is certainly one of its most distinctive strengths.

The management is in fact passive, although there is a non-negligible turnover of 52%, due to the monthly rebalancing, and to the fact that the fund still aims to maintain a low duration and a high distribution of holdings.

Holding distribution

This is certainly confirmed by the fact that it has 1,965 bonds in the portfolio, selected in the HY category, between minimum maturities of 18 months at issuance and ≥1 year at the time of rebalancing.

The result is a distribution mostly between 3–7 years:

  • 3–5 years: 41.95%
  • 5–7 years: 25.93%

and a quality rating breakdown, although below investment grade:

  • BB: 52.59%
  • B: 35.00%
  • CCC and below: 11.11%

It should also be said that, having a high weight in Consumer Cyclical (19.23%), this makes SPHY sensitive to the economic cycle (unemployment, consumer spending, recession).

Peer table

Starting from this base it is possible to make comparisons, and already by reading even just the reports shared by SPDR itself, there are very interesting comparisons in the SPDR Fixed Income Dashboard. And the other HY funds are JNK, HYMB and SJNK, listed in this peer table.

And the interesting thing that can be seen is that SPHY is still the least expensive, the most profitable in terms of total return, and the most competitive in terms of yield. But it is also the most diversified in terms of number of bonds and the one with the lowest duration in the HY SPDR category. This is because SPHY offers the best carry adjusted per unit of duration among the HY SPDRs. In other words: every point of interest rate risk taken by SPHY generates more return than the other HY ETFs.

Compared to other issuers

Both those in this peer table and the other passive ETFs that follow indices such as ICE BofA US High Yield, iBoxx USD High Yield and Bloomberg US Corporate HY can in my opinion be considered peers. And although it is not the one with the highest AUM, it remains very competitive in terms of TER but also performance; clearly there is no merit on the part of management, rather the advantage of a lower fee burden and a basket more suited to the market phase we have recently gone through.

What made it competitive?

This YTD 1Y rally of almost 7% is the result of an inflation compression, which played in favor of interest rate expectations, and an unexpected economic expansion, which stimulated the compression of the spread. Considering an OAD of 2.80, I am more inclined to weigh the second factor.

Considering that the OAS spread is 280 bps today, and that the average is above 400 bps, we can say that the compression of credit risk has indeed played a central role.

Now …

The point is this: we could simply say, the market risk premium is too low, it makes sense to move elsewhere. But that would be reductive; the risk premium measures riskiness, so the more it contracts, the more it means that the demand for these bonds is rising and (at least during the process) the total return goes up. To summarize this process I compare (a bit forcibly) the yield of SPHY and GOVT. In the last year, the yield of SPHY has contracted, while that of GOVT has risen. This process, personally, has in the past encouraged me to remain exposed to high yield, not discouraged me, despite the fact that the risk premium was falling.

Risk

What should be concerning, instead, is the inversion that is happening in this recent period in the trend of the spread. Returning to the comparison between SPHY and GOVT, you can see a weakening of the compression of SPHY’s yield (over 1 month it is increasing), while the growth rate of GOVT’s yield is also calming down. If this underlying trend continues, and if we consider that the average OAS is at least 200 bps higher, it is, in my opinion, reasonable to expect an increase in the risk premium. Especially considering the climate of tension that is developing in the market: it should be remembered that the CNN Fear & Greed Index is in an extreme greed phase, while the CDS of many big tech companies, primarily Oracle, are rising.

My opinion

It is true that there is room for a widening, following a repricing of risk and a potential increase in default rates. But it is also true that SPHY has a prevalence of BB ratings, so for a HY segment, it remains relatively “high quality.” So better than pure junk, something I keep away from in this period. In other words, I don’t think this is an exceptional period to build a position in high yield, even if it must be admitted that SPHY is a fairly borderline solution within the category.

Conclusion

For this reason I think the right rating remains HOLD. It remains a competitive ETF in the HY landscape, with a particularly competitive risk structure, duration, and, above all, costs. Personally, I look with suspicion at the HY segment in this period, but this does not make SPHY a bad ETF.

data.title: Lam Research: Fundamentals Remain Solid And I Remain Bullish
data.description:
In late June, I reiterated my strong buy rating on Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) on the basis of robust March quarter results, strong guidance, and undervaluation. As you can see in the rating history chart below, my optimism was well placed. The stock has gained over 60% since my update despite the recent pullback, and so I believe it's fair to say that Lam Research has been a strong outperformer. Today, we'll review their most recent earnings results to see whether the stock remains a strong buy.

Below, it is shown that Lam Research's fundamentals remain solid. Key financial metrics are robust, and the company's strategy seems to be working out well. December quarter guidance is a bit soft, but given the indication of strong demand in their September quarter, supply may be a constraint. The valuation has expanded since the low earlier in the year but remains notably off mid-2024 highs. I believe that's an appropriate reflection of the fundamentals, considering that the near-term outlook is a little mixed. Overall, Lam Research remains a solid business but due to the less attractive valuation, I have decided to downgrade to just a buy rating.

Record Revenues

In terms of the top line, it was another stellar quarter for Lam Research. They generated a total of $5.324 billion in revenues, which is a new record for the company. As you can see in the above chart, YoY growth did moderate a bit from the previous quarter, but overall, there is no reason to complain, as the 27.74% increase is the second strongest growth rate we have seen in the past three years at least. This is a clear demonstration of continued momentum in their business activity. As another positive sign, they were able to beat expectations by $93.62 million, and so their September quarter really was quite impressive.

China Reliance

Revenue results as a whole are robust, but there may be some concerns regarding where those revenues originate from. In the pie chart on the right, you can see that China made up 43% of revenues. That is a considerable jump from the previous quarter's 35% and even the prior year period's 37%. This reliance on China definitely poses some geopolitical risks, but with U.S. and China relations seemingly improving after tensions, I would say that this may not be a major threat to Lam Research's business at this point. That said, the political environment can be volatile and unpredictable, and so some cautiousness wouldn't be a bad idea.

Strategy Is Working

Before moving on to discuss other key financial metrics, I want to just briefly address the company's strategy. Lam Research has focused on expanding their product offerings, and some examples are shown above. As you can see, they are aiming to expand their product portfolio to include more of the chip manufacturing process, and from their record-breaking revenues and robust growth rate, I believe it is clear that the strategy implemented by management is working out very well. Technological innovation and product diversity are Lam Research's competitive advantages and will likely continue to drive growth moving forward.

A Well-Run Business

As stated above, the company is focused on expanding their product range, but that doesn't mean they aren't paying attention to operating efficiency. On the contrary, management also seems to be making notable improvements to profitability. As shown in the table above, gross margin expanded 30 basis points QoQ and in YoY terms there was an even greater expansion from 48.2%. Lam Research stated that this is a new gross margin record post-Novellus merger, and so their cost control is rock solid. Given their expertise in their space, it can also be inferred that pricing power remains strong for the company.

In total, operating expenses were reported to be $831.916 million, up 15.2% YoY. With revenue growth being near multiyear highs, this relatively smaller increase in operating expenses suggests superb efficiency and productivity, and operating margin expanded greatly as a result. As shown above, operating margin was reported to be 35%, up 60 basis points QoQ and a major improvement from the year ago quarter's 30.9%. This over 400 basis point YoY expansion is a clear indication of how well run Lam Research is currently. Furthermore, this operating margin is also a fresh record high for the company, and so the good news keeps coming.

For the bottom line, they reported an adjusted figure of $1.26, up 46.51% YoY. This is a more than respectable growth rate overall, but it does represent a slowdown from the previous quarter's 63.39% growth rate. That said, with the company hitting records for revenue, gross margin, and operating margin, I would say that this slowdown is unconcerning at this point, as business activity and efficiency are in strong positions.

Other Key Metrics

Like in my previous update, let's go over some of their other key metrics. Firstly, accounts receivable increased by quite an amount QoQ, and their days sales outstanding is also up from the previous quarter. Therefore, it seems that collections could have been better. This is probably the first notable hiccup in their results so far.

For inventory, you can see that there was a drop QoQ, and their inventory turns increased from 2.4 to 2.6. This is an indication that their products are flying off the shelves, as demand seems to be unrelenting at this point.

In terms of capital returns, shareholders may be a bit disappointed as both share repurchases and total dividend payout dropped QoQ. The more significant drop was in buybacks, and so that may indicate management believes the share price is less attractive than it has been.

The company does seem highly committed to future growth, as they increased their CAPEX QoQ, and perhaps even more importantly, they increased their headcount by 400 people to ~19,400. In an age where many companies are downsizing, this expansion in workforce shows they expect continued business momentum.

Near-Term Outlook

For their December quarter, Lam Research is expecting revenues of $5.2 billion at the midpoint, representing an increase of 18.83% YoY. That would be another dip in top-line growth, and so perhaps there may be some indications that business activity is slowing. However, given the QoQ inventory decline and inventory turns increase discussed above, demand doesn't seem to be a problem. Therefore, there may be signs that supply is constraining growth.

For gross and operating margin, the above midpoints show that expansion will continue, but the improvements will likely be less than what we saw in their September quarter. Therefore, efficiency and productivity improvements may be showing some signs of plateauing.

Lastly, they project an EPS midpoint of $1.15, implying a 26.37% YoY growth rate. That is quite a deceleration from the September quarter as well, but that isn't too surprising considering the expected top line deceleration and plateauing of margin expansion. Overall, I would say that the near term outlook is slightly disappointing, but business is expected to remain quite solid.

Valuation Evaluation

The P/E ratio for Lam Research has rebounded significantly from the lows seen earlier this year, but it also remains a ways below the peak of mid-2024. Overall, I would say that the strong fundamentals justify the stock's valuation.

In today's analysis, the September quarter was shown to be very strong. The top line, margins, and other key metrics were, for the most part, very promising. As stated above, December quarter guidance is a bit softer than desired, but with stats showing demand being strong, supply may be a constraint.

Nonetheless, I believe the slightly mixed near-term outlook also justifies the fact that the valuation is below the mid-2024 high. As mentioned earlier, Lam Research slowed their share repurchases as a sign that the stock may now be less attractively priced, and so, in my view, the valuation is an appropriate reflection of their fundamentals.

Conclusion

Buying a robust business at a fair price isn't that bad of an idea, and I believe this applies to Lam Research. While the valuation is less attractive than it was earlier this year, the fundamentals are overall still very favorable. As stated above, September quarter results were very solid, and while December quarter guidance is a bit shaky, it seems that some supply constraints could be a factor moving forward. Overall, I remain bullish on Lam Research, but due to the somewhat lowered risk/reward, I have decided to downgrade to a buy rating for now.

data.title: UTG Vs. UTF: The Battle Of U.S. Utilities Against Global Infrastructure
data.description:
Investment thesis

In this paper, I would like to analyze two major funds whose strategy focuses on investing in utility and infrastructure securities. These funds are Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG) and Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF). These assets' investment potential is tied to the Fed's likely continued rate cuts. Because of that, leveraged funds like UTG and UTF will get an extra boost compared to other funds investing in utilities and infrastructure. To me, the two funds represent a classic battle between the US utilities sector and global infrastructure. Choosing between the two assets, I would go with UTG. UTG's main difference is its focus on the US market, whereas UTF focuses on global infrastructure.

UTF's investment portfolio is more geographically diversified and invests in cellular communications facilities, road infrastructure, airports, pipelines, etc. That said, in addition to UTG and UTF, there are some other funds that can be seen as direct and indirect competitors. For instance, there is the DNP Select Income Fund (DNP), which historically trades at a very high premium to NAV, thus reflecting the value of its stability. In the same vein, it is more expensive because of this, making investments in DNP less attractive at present. The other fund is BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (BUI), which features no leverage, as its managers use a covered options selling strategy to generate income. Because of this, BUI is less volatile, but it also limits its upside potential in a bull market. The main drawback of these funds in the context of the Fed's rate cuts is the lack of credit leverage, which for UTG and UTF provides the potential for additional gains.

Fundamental similarities between UTG and UTF and their main differences

As I said in the introduction to this article, UTG and UTF are the most popular closed-end funds investing in utilities and infrastructure assets. Many investors therefore consider them to be interchangeable, though there are important differences between their strategies. Defining their similar qualities, it should be noted that these funds pay dividends monthly and use leverage to generate high returns. Leverage levels are highest at 30% (in the case of UTF) and 20% (in the case of UTG). Importantly, both funds have a high level of reliability, demonstrate a long history of payments without reductions (especially UTG), and receive excellent reputation ratings from investors.

But a deeper look at the basic similarities between UTG and UTF shows three main differences. All of them are related to the investment strategy outlined in the fund prospectuses.

The first criteria is the structure of assets in the portfolio. In case of UTG, a conservative portfolio is formed (here, it means that the portfolio distributes capital among the assets of utility companies that have a protective mechanism due to the inelasticity of demand for their services, thus ensuring a stable cash flow), which is focused on securities of US utility companies. They include electric power companies, gas companies, water supply companies, and telecommunications companies. The fund managers justify this choice by saying that they are betting on assets in the defensive sector of the US economy, which people need at all times (to meet their basic needs), irrespective of the macroeconomic situation. The UTF is a broader fund that invests more in securities of companies involved in global infrastructure. There are also utility assets here, but the majority of the portfolio is made up of shares in companies involved in pipelines, toll roads, and rail transport. The UTF managers are betting more on economic activity and the development of logistics, due to which economic cyclicality factors affect the fund's financial results.

The second criteria is the geography of investments, which as I have already noted, is different only in that UTG invests more heavily in US companies, and UTF also invests in assets that position themselves as international organizations (companies from Europe, Canada, and Australia). Below is the geographical structure of the UTF portfolio.

A third criterion is the differences in volatility and returns between funds. I think UTG is more attractive because it's calmer and safer during times of economic turbulence and financial instability in the markets. This is for example because demand for stuff like electricity doesn't drop as much as demand for fuel or freight. But, UTF has the potential to show higher growth when the market is on an upward trend. But, the global economy has become too structured and unstable after deglobalization, which is hurting companies involved in infrastructure projects.

To justify this argument, we need to take into account that in 2025, infrastructure companies around the world faced stricter requirements from governments introducing localization rules. For instance, it is becoming more difficult for overseas infrastructure companies to enter new regional markets (for example, companies from Europe are facing difficulties in securing new contracts in China due to geopolitics and in the US due to the Buy American Act). Infrastructure companies are also finding it difficult to optimize costs due to the rising cost of supply chains, increasing capital expenditures and the cost of raw materials and technology. And considering that many infrastructure projects abroad are financed through public-private partnerships, the problems of record budget deficits and high debt payments are reducing these budget expenditures, thus negatively affecting infrastructure development.

Comparative table of UTG and UTF fund characteristics

The following is a brief comparative table based on key criteria, to help you understand the difference between UTG and UTF.
